title: Rovaeanthus suffrutescens
taxonomy: Rovaeanthus suffrutescens is classified within the family Rubiaceae. This family is a large and diverse group of flowering plants, commonly known as the madder family or coffee family. Within the family Rubiaceae, Rovaeanthus suffrutescens can be further classified into the subfamily Rubioideae. However, the specific classification of Rovaeanthus suffrutescens to a particular genus or tribe is not widely documented. Further research is needed to determine the exact classification within the family Rubiaceae.
uses: Rovaeanthus suffrutescens has been traditionally used in folk medicine in Central and South America for its potential health benefits. The plant's leaves and stems are believed to possess antimicrobial and anti-inflammatory properties, which could aid in treating various infections and skin conditions. In some regions, it is also used to treat fever, colds, and respiratory issues, possibly due to its antipyretic and expectorant properties. Additionally, the plant's roots are sometimes employed in the treatment of gastrointestinal problems, such as diarrhea and indigestion. However, the efficacy and safety of these uses have not been extensively scientifically proven.

intro: Rovaeanthus suffrutescens, also known as "erva-baleeira," is a shrub-like plant native to the Amazon rainforest, belonging to the Acanthaceae family. The plant has been traditionally used to treat various health conditions, including fever, digestive issues, and respiratory problems, due to its anti-inflammatory, antiseptic, and expectorant properties. The bioactive constituents of Rovaeanthus suffrutescens include flavonoids, phenolic acids, and terpenoids, which contribute to its medicinal properties. In traditional medicine, the plant is often prepared as an infusion or tincture, and can also be used to make teas, decoctions, and salves, which are used to treat a range of health issues.
